documentation.\n\n\ntext-wrap\n-----------------------------------------------------------\nClassStylestext-wraptext-wrap: wrap;text-nowraptext-wrap: nowrap;text-balancetext-wrap: balance;text-prettytext-wrap: pretty;\nExamples\nAllowing text to wrap\nUse the text-wrap utility to wrap overflowing text onto multiple lines at logical points in the text:\nBeloved Manhattan soup stand closesNew Yorkers are facing the winter chill with less warmth this year as the city's most revered soup stand unexpectedly shutters, following a series of events that have left the community puzzled.<article class=\"text-wrap\">  <h3>Beloved Manhattan soup stand closes</h3>  <p>New Yorkers are facing the winter chill...</p></article>\nPreventing text from wrapping\nUse the text-nowrap utility to prevent text from wrapping, allowing it to overflow if necessary:\nBeloved Manhattan soup stand closesNew Yorkers are facing the winter chill with less warmth this year as the city's most revered soup stand unexpectedly shutters, following a series of events that have left the community puzzled.<article class=\"text-nowrap\">  <h3>Beloved Manhattan soup stand closes</h3>  <p>New Yorkers are facing the winter chill...</p></article>\nBalanced text wrapping\nUse the text-balance utility to distribute the text evenly across each line:\nBeloved Manhattan soup stand closesNew Yorkers are facing the winter chill with less warmth this year as the city's most revered soup stand unexpectedly shutters, following a series of events that have left the community puzzled.<article>  <h3 class=\"text-balance\">Beloved Manhattan soup stand closes</h3>  <p>New Yorkers are facing the winter chill...</p></article>\nFor performance reasons browsers limit text balancing to blocks that are ~6 lines or less, making it best suited for headings.\nPretty text wrapping\nUse the text-pretty utility to prevent orphans (a single word on its own line) at the end of a text block:\nBeloved Manhattan soup stand closesNew Yorkers are facing the winter chill with less warmth this year as the city's most revered soup stand unexpectedly shutters, following a series of events that have left the community puzzled.<article>  <h3 class=\"text-pretty\">Beloved Manhattan soup stand closes</h3>  <p>New Yorkers are facing the winter chill...</p></article>\nResponsive design\nPrefix a text-wrap utility with a breakpoint variant like md: to only apply the utility at medium screen sizes and above:<h1 class=\"text-pretty md:text-balance ...\">  <!-- ... --></h1>Learn more about using variants in the variants documentation.\n\n\ntheme\n-----------------------------------------------------------\nOverview\nTailwind is a framework for building custom designs, and different designs need different typography, colors, shadows, breakpoints, and more.\nThese low-level design decisions are often called design tokens, and in Tailwind projects you store those values in theme variables.\nWhat are theme variables?\nTheme variables are special CSS variables defined using the @theme directive that influence which utility classes exist in your project.\nFor example, you can add a new color to your project by defining a theme variable like --color-mint-500:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme {  --color-mint-500: oklch(0.72 0.11 178);}\nNow you can use utility classes like bg-mint-500, text-mint-500, or fill-mint-500 in your HTML:\nHTML<div class=\"bg-mint-500\">  <!-- ... --></div>\nTailwind also generates regular CSS variables for your theme variables so you can reference your design tokens in arbitrary values or inline styles:\nHTML<div style=\"background-color: var(--color-mint-500)\">  <!-- ... --></div>\nLearn more about how theme variables map to different utility classes in the theme variable namespaces documentation.\nWhy @theme instead of :root?\nTheme variables aren't just CSS variables — they also instruct Tailwind to create new utility classes that you can use in your HTML.\nSince they do more than regular CSS variables, Tailwind uses special syntax so that defining theme variables is always explicit. Theme variables are also required to be defined top-level and not nested under other selectors or media queries, and using a special syntax makes it possible to enforce that.\nDefining regular CSS variables with :root can still be useful in Tailwind projects when you want to define a variable that isn't meant to be connected to a utility class. Use @theme when you want a design token to map directly to a utility class, and use :root for defining regular CSS variables that shouldn't have corresponding utility classes.\nRelationship to utility classes\nSome utility classes in Tailwind like flex and object-cover are static, and are always the same from project to project. But many others are driven by theme variables, and only exist because of the theme variables you've defined.\nFor example, theme variables defined in the --font-* namespace determine all of the font-family utilities that exist in a project:\n./node_modules/tailwindcss/theme.css@theme {  --font-sans: ui-sans-serif, system-ui, sans-serif, \"Apple Color Emoji\", \"Segoe UI Emoji\", \"Segoe UI Symbol\", \"Noto Color Emoji\";  --font-serif: ui-serif, Georgia, Cambria, \"Times New Roman\", Times, serif;  --font-mono: ui-monospace, SFMono-Regular, Menlo, Monaco, Consolas, \"Liberation Mono\", \"Courier New\", monospace;  /* ... */}\nThe font-sans, font-serif, and font-mono utilities only exist by default because Tailwind's default theme defines the --font-sans, --font-serif, and --font-mono theme variables.\nIf another theme variable like --font-poppins were defined, a font-poppins utility class would become available to go with it:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme {  --font-poppins: Poppins, sans-serif;}\nHTML<h1 class=\"font-poppins\">This headline will use Poppins.</h1>\nYou can name your theme variables whatever you want within these namespaces, and a corresponding utility with the same name will become available to use in your HTML.\nRelationship to variants\nSome theme variables are used to define variants rather than utilities. For example theme variables in the --breakpoint-* namespace determine which responsive breakpoint variants exist in your project:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme {  --breakpoint-3xl: 120rem;}\nNow you can use the 3xl:* variant to only trigger a utility when the viewport is 120rem or wider:\nHTML<div class=\"3xl:grid-cols-6 grid grid-cols-2 md:grid-cols-4\">  <!-- ... --></div>\nLearn more about how theme variables map to different utility classes and variants in the theme variable namespaces documentation.\nTheme variable namespaces\nTheme variables are defined in namespaces and each namespace corresponds to one or more utility class or variant APIs.\nDefining new theme variables in these namespaces will make new corresponding utilities and variants available in your project:\nNamespaceUtility classes--color-*Color utilities like bg-red-500, text-sky-300, and many more--font-*Font family utilities like font-sans--text-*Font size utilities like text-xl--font-weight-*Font weight utilities like font-bold--tracking-*Letter spacing utilities like tracking-wide--leading-*Line height utilities like leading-tight--breakpoint-*Responsive breakpoint variants like sm:*--container-*Container query variants like @sm:* and size utilities like max-w-md--spacing-*Spacing and sizing utilities like px-4, max-h-16, and many more--radius-*Border radius utilities like rounded-sm--shadow-*Box shadow utilities like shadow-md--inset-shadow-*Inset box shadow utilities like inset-shadow-xs--drop-shadow-*Drop shadow filter utilities like drop-shadow-md--blur-*Blur filter utilities like blur-md--perspective-*Perspective utilities like perspective-near--aspect-*Aspect ratio utilities like aspect-video--ease-*Transition timing function utilities like ease-out--animate-*Animation utilities like animate-spin\nFor a list of all of the default theme variables, see the default theme variable reference.\nDefault theme variables\nWhen you import tailwindcss at the top of your CSS file, it includes a set of default theme variables to get you started.\nHere's what you're actually importing when you import tailwindcss:\nnode_modules/tailwindcss/index.css@layer theme, base, components, utilities;@import \"./theme.css\" layer(theme);@import \"./preflight.css\" layer(base);@import \"./utilities.css\" layer(utilities);\nThat theme.css file includes the default color palette, type scale, shadows, fonts, and more:\nnode_modules/tailwindcss/theme.css@theme {  --font-sans: ui-sans-serif, system-ui, sans-serif, \"Apple Color Emoji\", \"Segoe UI Emoji\", \"Segoe UI Symbol\", \"Noto Color Emoji\";  --font-serif: ui-serif, Georgia, Cambria, \"Times New Roman\", Times, serif;  --font-mono: ui-monospace, SFMono-Regular, Menlo, Monaco, Consolas, \"Liberation Mono\", \"Courier New\", monospace;  --color-red-50: oklch(0.971 0.013 17.38);  --color-red-100: oklch(0.936 0.032 17.717);  --color-red-200: oklch(0.885 0.062 18.334);  /* ... */  --shadow-2xs: 0 1px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.05);  --shadow-xs: 0 1px 2px 0 rgb(0 0 0 / 0.05);  --shadow-sm: 0 1px 3px 0 rgb(0 0 0 / 0.1), 0 1px 2px -1px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.1);  /* ... */}\nThis is why utilities like bg-red-200, font-serif, and shadow-sm exist out of the box — they're driven by the default theme, not hardcoded into the framework like flex-col or pointer-events-none.\nFor a list of all of the default theme variables, see the default theme variable reference.\nCustomizing your theme\nThe default theme variables are very general purpose and suitable for building dramatically different designs, but they are still just a starting point. It's very common to customize things like the color palette, fonts, and shadows to build exactly the design you have in mind.\nExtending the default theme\nUse @theme to define new theme variables and extend the default theme:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme {  --font-script: Great Vibes, cursive;}\nThis makes a new font-script utility class available that you can use in your HTML, just like the default font-sans or font-mono utilities:\nHTML<p class=\"font-script\">This will use the Great Vibes font family.</p>\nLearn more about how theme variables map to different utility classes and variants in the theme variable namespaces documentation.\nOverriding the default theme\nOverride a default theme variable value by redefining it within @theme:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme {  --breakpoint-sm: 30rem;}\nNow the sm:* variant will trigger at 30rem instead of the default 40rem viewport size:\nHTML<div class=\"grid grid-cols-1 sm:grid-cols-3\">  <!-- ... --></div>\nTo completely override an entire namespace in the default theme, set the entire namespace to initial using the special asterisk syntax:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme {  --color-*: initial;  --color-white: #fff;  --color-purple: #3f3cbb;  --color-midnight: #121063;  --color-tahiti: #3ab7bf;  --color-bermuda: #78dcca;}\nWhen you do this, all of the default utilities that use that namespace (like bg-red-500) will be removed, and only your custom values (like bg-midnight) will be available.\nLearn more about how theme variables map to different utility classes and variants in the theme variable namespaces documentation.\nUsing a custom theme\nTo completely disable the default theme and use only custom values, set the global theme variable namespace to initial:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme {  --*: initial;  --spacing: 4px;  --font-body: Inter, sans-serif;  --color-lagoon: oklch(0.72 0.11 221.19);  --color-coral: oklch(0.74 0.17 40.24);  --color-driftwood: oklch(0.79 0.06 74.59);  --color-tide: oklch(0.49 0.08 205.88);  --color-dusk: oklch(0.82 0.15 72.09);}\nNow none of the default utility classes that are driven by theme variables will be available, and you'll only be able to use utility classes matching your custom theme variables like font-body and text-dusk.\nDefining animation keyframes\nDefine the @keyframes rules for your --animate-* theme variables within @theme to include them in your generated CSS:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme {  --animate-fade-in-scale: fade-in-scale 0.3s ease-out;  @keyframes fade-in-scale {    0% {      opacity: 0;      transform: scale(0.95);    }    100% {      opacity: 1;      transform: scale(1);    }  }}\nIf you want your custom @keyframes rules to always be included even when not adding an --animate-* theme variable, define them outside of @theme instead.\nReferencing other variables\nWhen defining theme variables that reference other variables, use the inline option:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme inline {  --font-sans: var(--font-inter);}\nUsing the inline option, the utility class will use the theme variable value instead of referencing the actual theme variable:\ndist.css.font-sans {  font-family: var(--font-inter);}\nWithout using inline, your utility classes might resolve to unexpected values because of how variables are resolved in CSS.\nFor example, this text will fall back to sans-serif instead of using Inter like you might expect:\nHTML<div id=\"parent\" style=\"--font-sans: var(--font-inter, sans-serif);\">  <div id=\"child\" style=\"--font-inter: Inter; font-family: var(--font-sans);\">    This text will use the sans-serif font, not Inter.  </div></div>\nThis happens because var(--font-sans) is resolved where --font-sans is defined (on #parent), and --font-inter has no value there since it's not defined until deeper in the tree (on #child).\nGenerating all CSS variables\nBy default only used CSS variables will be generated in the final CSS output. If you want to always generate all CSS variables, you can use the static theme option:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@theme static {  --color-primary: var(--color-red-500);  --color-secondary: var(--color-blue-500);}\nSharing across projects\nSince theme variables are defined in CSS, sharing them across projects is just a matter of throwing them into their own CSS file that you can import in each project:\n./packages/brand/theme.css@theme {  --*: initial;  --spacing: 4px;  --font-body: Inter, sans-serif;  --color-lagoon: oklch(0.72 0.11 221.19);  --color-coral: oklch(0.74 0.17 40.24);  --color-driftwood: oklch(0.79 0.06 74.59);  --color-tide: oklch(0.49 0.08 205.88);  --color-dusk: oklch(0.82 0.15 72.09);}\nThen you can use @import to include your theme variables in other projects:\n./packages/admin/app.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@import \"../brand/theme.css\";\nYou can put shared theme variables like this in their own package in monorepo setups or even publish them to NPM and import them just like any other third-party CSS files.\nUsing your theme variables\nAll of your theme variables are turned into regular CSS variables when you compile your CSS:\ndist.css:root {  --font-sans: ui-sans-serif, system-ui, sans-serif, \"Apple Color Emoji\", \"Segoe UI Emoji\", \"Segoe UI Symbol\", \"Noto Color Emoji\";  --font-serif: ui-serif, Georgia, Cambria, \"Times New Roman\", Times, serif;  --font-mono: ui-monospace, SFMono-Regular, Menlo, Monaco, Consolas, \"Liberation Mono\", \"Courier New\", monospace;  --color-red-50: oklch(0.971 0.013 17.38);  --color-red-100: oklch(0.936 0.032 17.717);  --color-red-200: oklch(0.885 0.062 18.334);  /* ... */  --shadow-2xs: 0 1px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.05);  --shadow-xs: 0 1px 2px 0 rgb(0 0 0 / 0.05);  --shadow-sm: 0 1px 3px 0 rgb(0 0 0 / 0.1), 0 1px 2px -1px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.1);  /* ... */}\nThis makes it easy to reference all of your design tokens in any of your custom CSS or inline styles.\nWith custom CSS\nUse your theme variables to get access to your design tokens when you're writing custom CSS that needs to use the same values:\napp.css@import \"tailwindcss\";@layer components {  .typography {    p {      font-size: var(--text-base);      color: var(--color-gray-700);    }    h1 {      font-size: var(--text-2xl--line-height);      font-weight: var(--font-weight-semibold);      color: var(--color-gray-950);    }    h2 {      font-size: var(--text-xl);      font-weight: var(--font-weight-semibold);      color: var(--color-gray-950);    }  }}\nThis is often useful when styling HTML you don't control, like Markdown content coming from a database or API and rendered to HTML.\nWith arbitrary values\nUsing theme variables in arbitrary values can be useful, especially in combination with the calc() function.\nHTML<div class=\"relative rounded-xl\">  <div class=\"absolute inset-px rounded-[calc(var(--radius-xl)-1px)]\">    <!-- ... -->  </div>  <!-- ... --></div>\nIn the above example, we're subtracting 1px from the --radius-xl value on a nested inset element to make sure it has a concentric border radius.\nReferencing in JavaScript\nMost of the time when you need to reference your theme variables in JS you can just use the CSS variables directly, just like any other CSS value.\nFor example, the popular Motion library for React lets you animate to and from CSS variable values:\nJSX<motion.div animate={{ backgroundColor: \"var(--color-blue-500)\" }} />\nIf you need access to a resolved CSS variable value in JS, you can use getComputedStyle to get the value of a theme variable on the document root:\nspaghetti.jslet styles = getComputedStyle(document.documentElement);let shadow = styles.getPropertyValue(\"--shadow-xl\");\nDefault theme variable reference\nFor reference, here's a complete list of the theme variables included by default when you import Tailwind CSS into your project:\ntailwindcss/theme.css@theme {  --font-sans: ui-sans-serif, system-ui, sans-serif, \"Apple Color Emoji\", \"Segoe UI Emoji\", \"Segoe UI Symbol\", \"Noto Color Emoji\";  --font-serif: ui-serif, Georgia, Cambria, \"Times New Roman\", Times, serif;  --font-mono:
